title: Organised violence is a trade, whether legal or illegal | Aeon Essays
gist: Drawing on prison interviews with Mexican cartel hitmen and Charles Tilly's 'violence specialist' framework, the essay argues organised violence (cartel, police, military, mercenary) is a single labor market driven by wages and prestige, and that cartels now out-bid states for fighters across Latin America while private military firms globalize licit violence work.

insights.claim: Soldiers, police, criminals, guerrillas, terrorists and génocidaires are all 'violence specialists' (Charles Tilly's term) performing the same trade of trained killing for a living, blurring the assumed good-vs-evil divide.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Reframing violence as fungible skilled labor rather than morality predicts that talent flows to the highest bidder across legal/illegal lines.
insights.quote: all these people, primarily men, are what the sociologist Charles Tilly called ‘violence specialists’
insights.locator: Whether they are on the payroll

insights.claim: Imprisoned Mexican hitmen consistently described killing as 'chambear' (to work), treating gang violence and homicide as everyday employment rather than deviance.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Field evidence that recruitment is an economic decision, implying labor-market interventions (jobs, wages) matter more than moral 'cures' for crime.
insights.quote: Most of them were using chambear (Spanish-language vernacular for the verb ‘to work’) to talk about gang violence and killings.

insights.claim: Sociologists classify high-risk violence work as 'edgework' offering both danger and thrill, and practitioners develop a psychological profile of desensitisation and adrenaline-seeking.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Explains the non-monetary pull (prestige, thrill, masculinity) that supplements pay in sustaining violence-labor supply.
insights.quote: Sociologists call this type of profession edgework
insights.locator: Regardless of whether they are American

themes.theme: Shared recruitment of state and gang

insights.claim: Governments and gang leaders use the same recruitment techniques, promising young men respect, good salary and a larger cause, undercutting the deviant-vs-altruist distinction between criminals and soldiers.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Implies state and cartel compete in the same recruitment pool, so state capacity erodes when it cannot match the offer.
insights.quote: Both clandestine organisations and government agencies tell young boys that their peers and families will respect them, that they will earn a good salary, and that they will contribute to a larger cause.

insights.claim: Los Zetas, Mexico's deadliest cartel, was founded by 30+ defecting GAFES special-forces soldiers in 1997, showing state-trained killing skills are directly transferable to illegal organisations.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: State investment in elite violence training can leak into and upgrade criminal organisations, a structural risk of militarized counterinsurgency.
insights.quote: more than 30 soldiers of the Special Forces Airmobile Group (GAFES in Spanish) defected to work for the leader of the Gulf Cartel

insights.claim: Across Latin America and the Caribbean, drug cartels are winning the recruitment competition by offering wages national armies and police cannot match.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Wage competition for fighters is a leading indicator of state-monopoly-on-violence erosion and rising instability risk in the region.
insights.quote: drug cartels are winning the same competition. They offer wages that national armies and police forces cannot match.

insights.claim: An imprisoned hitman earned ~10,000 pesos (under US$600) per killing, roughly a Mexican police officer's monthly salary, quantifying the wage gap that pulls labor toward cartels.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Concrete per-kill wage benchmark shows why illicit violence work out-competes formal employment for the rural working class.
insights.quote: he would earn 10,000 pesos (just under US$600) per ‘job’ – killing someone – which was roughly what a police officer in Mexico might earn in a month

insights.claim: More than 3 million people were murdered in Latin America and the Caribbean between 1990 and 2021 (per UNODC), most of them young men, quantifying the scale of the regional violence labor market and its human cost.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
entities: UN Office on Drugs and Crime
insights.so_what: Scale of lost young-male labor is a chronic demographic and economic drag on the region, beyond the immediate security toll.
insights.quote: more than 3 million people were murdered in Latin America and the Caribbean between 1990 and 2021, most of them young men
insights.locator: The young men I interviewed were part

insights.claim: The modern state's legitimate monopoly on violence emerged with Napoleon offering recruits careers, purpose and the idea of defending the people rather than a king, solving Machiavelli's problem of disloyal mercenaries.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: The state monopoly on violence is a historically contingent bargain (pay plus purpose), not a permanent fact, so it can be reversed.
insights.quote: the notion that a modern state holds a legitimate monopoly on violence on behalf of its citizens, a foundational idea of today’s modern state
insights.locator: That began to change in the early 19th

insights.claim: States collapse when they can't pay their fighters: the Russian, French and Chinese revolutions each broke out partly because governments could no longer afford their armies.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Fiscal capacity to pay security forces is a direct predictor of regime survival, a macro/geopolitical risk signal.
insights.quote: States fall when they can’t pay their fighters.

insights.claim: Private licit violence work, once limited to bouncers and bodyguards, is now a global business via firms like Blackwater (US/Iraq) and Wagner Group (Russia/Crimea, Ukraine, Africa).
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: A growing, globalized private-military market lets states outsource force and avoid accountability, a structural geopolitical and ESG-risk trend.
insights.quote: private ‘licit’ violence work, once reserved for bar bouncers or bodyguards, is now a global business
insights.locator: The rise of companies such as

insights.claim: In Iraq in 2008 the US Department of Defense employed over 155,000 contractors, more than the actual troops deployed, with the explicit benefit of avoiding accountability for its own forces.
insights.speaker: Raúl Zepeda Gil
insights.so_what: Contractors outnumbering troops quantifies the scale of state reliance on outsourced violence and the accountability gap it creates.
insights.quote: the US defence department employed in excess of 155,000 contractors, more than the actual troops deployed
